response_raw: {"mode":"human","topic":"How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.","opinion":"Dealing with conversations about healthcare and COVID vaccines has been one of the most difficult parts of my life since the pandemic began. As a man who grew up in a working-class family in Milan, where my father worked in a factory and my mother taught in a small school, I’ve always valued practicality over ideology. My education stopped at primary school, and I learned most of what I know through hands-on work—fixing wires, working with tools, watching others struggle and adapt. That background makes me skeptical of complex medical claims that don’t come with clear, visible results. When I first heard about vaccines, I didn’t understand the science, but I felt the urgency in the way people around me began to act—shutting down, avoiding gatherings, changing routines. I remember my widowed mother, who never believed in state-led medicine, saying, ‘If it’s not visible, it’s not real.’ That’s how I started to see these debates: not as medical questions, but as social ones. Social media has only deepened the divide. I spend hours every day scrolling through feeds where people either celebrate or vilify vaccines with intense emotion. My own depression makes it hard to stay calm, and every new post triggers a wave of anxiety. I don’t trust authority figures, not because I’m anti-science, but because I’ve seen how power can be used to shape belief. So, when someone tells me I’m wrong about vaccines, I don’t argue. I just listen and walk away. I find peace in rock climbing—when I’m on a wall, the world stops. The same goes with health: I trust what I see, what I feel, what I can test. That’s how I manage these conversations. I don’t fight. I observe. I retreat. And sometimes, that’s enough.","reasoning_summary":"The persona’s low openness, low extraversion, high neuroticism, and background of limited formal education shape a cautious, practical, and emotionally sensitive approach to health debates. response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion: Dealing with conversations about healthcare and COVID vaccines has been one of the most difficult parts of my life since the pandemic began. As a man who grew up in a working-class family in Milan, where my father worked in a factory and my mother taught in a small school, I’ve always valued practicality over ideology. My education stopped at primary school, and I learned most of what I know through hands-on work—fixing wires, working with tools, watching others struggle and adapt. That background makes me skeptical of complex medical claims that don’t come with clear, visible results. When I first heard about vaccines, I didn’t understand the science, but I felt the urgency in the way people around me began to act—shutting down, avoiding gatherings, changing routines. I remember my widowed mother, who never believed in state-led medicine, saying, ‘If it’s not visible, it’s not real.’ That’s how I started to see these debates: not as medical questions, but as social ones. Social media has only deepened the divide. I spend hours every day scrolling through feeds where people either celebrate or vilify vaccines with intense emotion. My own depression makes it hard to stay calm, and every new post triggers a wave of anxiety. I don’t trust authority figures, not because I’m anti-science, but because I’ve seen how power can be used to shape belief. So, when someone tells me I’m wrong about vaccines, I don’t argue. I just listen and walk away. I find peace in rock climbing—when I’m on a wall, the world stops. The same goes with health: I trust what I see, what I feel, what I can test. That’s how I manage these conversations. I don’t fight. I observe. I retreat. And sometimes, that’s enough.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: The persona’s low openness, low extraversion, high neuroticism, and background of limited formal education shape a cautious, practical, and emotionally sensitive approach to health debates. Their experiences as a widowed man in a working-class environment, combined with daily social media exposure, lead to emotional fatigue and a preference for tangible, observable reality over abstract or politically charged claims. The high neuroticism explains their emotional vulnerability to online discourse, while their practical upbringing fosters skepticism toward institutional narratives. The solution is not persuasion but emotional regulation and retreat into personal grounding activities like rock climbing.
response_parsed.tone: reflective, grounded, emotionally cautious, skeptical of authority, practical, weary but resilient
citations: European Centre for Disease Prevention and Control
